Hey, welcome back again and welcome to the module on prospecting, the science of sifting through a very large pool of prospective buyers to quickly identify the potentially real ones without pissing any of them off along the way, which is actually easier said than done. What does that mean? Well, the best way to explain this theory is to jump right into the deep end of the pool, so to speak, by showing you what I consider to be by far the most compelling and certainly the best acted scene in the entire Wolf of Wall Street movie. Let's cut to that right now. Check out how well acted and amazing this scene is. So I want you right now to give a warm Auckland, New Zealand welcome for my good friend and the world's greatest sales trainer, Mr Jordan Bayford. Thank you. And there you have it, the best acted scene in the Wolf of Wall Street movie, but I had to show it to you anyway because I think it's pretty cool. I got my cameo for my legacy for my family in the world and there you have it. Anyway, what I really want to show you here is what happens next when Leo pulls out the pen, right, and does that famous scene, walks to a panic-stricken New Zealander in that seminar room and he says, sell me this pen. It's, it's a amazing pen for professionals to sell me this pen. It's a nice pen. Let me cut to the chase. After the movie came out, something I'd done maybe five or 10 times before the movie, right, just occasionally became this like public thing where everybody, everyone just said, hey, sell me this pen if you're the first person that ever asked me that question. Let me tell you the bottom line here. I'm going to show you how to sell someone a pen that will not just blow them away, but really let them know that you truly are the real deal when it comes to sales because here's the bottom line. When someone says to you, sell me this pen, there's only one irrational response you give them. How long have you been in the market for a pen? Like, are you looking to buy a pen? If someone says to me, sell me this pen, how do I know? It's a real world exercise. How do I know that this person is in the market for a pen? You see, as a straight line sales person, the first rule is that we don't make presentations to people who are not needing what we're selling, who have the money to buy what we're selling, and who we can help. I mean, people, we can fill that need and take them out of pain, so to speak, and put them in a position where they feel pleasure or they're back in control of their lives. Let me ask you this simple, honest question. How could you possibly know if someone that you're speaking to needs what you're selling? In other words, you could be the greatest sales person. Well, you could be Jordan Dolfwood number two, right? You really have a down pat. You can make the greatest presentation. You know how to make something sound sexy, make it sound cool, cheap, great deal, but they don't need what you're selling or they're broke. They have no money. Now, if they need it, they can't afford it. Why would you want to waste your time going through a whole full blown straight line presentation to strip the whole thing, right? There's no way they could buy from you, even if they wanted to, or they just have no desire for what you're selling. This is what prospecting is all about. It's about essentially asking people a series of questions in a way that actually builds rapport, makes them say, wow, this person really cares. That's why you're asking them questions. You're not just going to sell them, proc. You're going to ask them to find out information that's relevant, germane to the topic at hand, which is, I have a pen for sale. So in other words, the first thing you would do is say, so tell me how long you've been in the market for a pen. Now, if the person is sharp shooting you, they want to kind of mess you up and say, oh, I'm not. I say, oh, great, have a nice day. I don't sell people if people don't need it. They'll say, wow, so you're a real salesperson. In other words, a real salesperson would say, oh, great, then have a good day, or even better still, you could all say, oh, do you know anyone that's in the market for a pen if you wanted to really sharp shoot them? The point is you would never just simply start saying, this is the best pen. This pen writes down. This pen writes never to ink. It writes on the moon. It writes unbelievable. It's the best pen. What a deal this pen is. This pen is cheap. This pen is cost-effective. It's worth 10,000. They'll say, what the hell? I don't need a pen, or I have all the pens I need right now. They'll be calling me in two years when I'm out of my pens. How could you possibly sell anything to anyone unless you know number one, do they need it? B, what's their price point? C, what pens have they used in the past? Is it for business or personal reasons? Right? Are they looking for one pen, 20 pens? Is it for their office? Is it a home for a special occasion? I mean think about this logically. Anyone that would try to sell you a pen or an insurance policy or a car, imagine this. You go to the car dealer, I like to buy a car. Here's your car. Let me tell you about this car. They'll say, well, do I have a family? How many people are going to be in the car? Do I care about gas mileage? Is that important to me? Have I any cars I have right now that are filling with, what have I liked in the past? Have I bought a car I loved? Anything to hate about a car? How could you possibly sell anything to anyone without first finding out all the things that are relevant to that person? And then based on what they say, then you tailor your presentation to directly suit their needs. That's what I meant about the trusted advisor, the family friend, the family doctor. Even as you go to the doctor, what's the first thing he does? He puts you through a series of questions and exams to find out what's ailing you. What's your problem? How's your past health been? It's really an excellent parallel. And when the doctor does that, he's not talking about the price of tea in China. He's not going off to Pluto or your anus. He's asking you relevant questions so we can help you get what you want, which is what? Better health. Feel better. Not be sick anymore. You don't just walk and he goes, okay, here's a prescription for, here's some penicillin you say, what the heck? That's the worst doctor in the world. In fact, I'll tell you a great story. I once had that very thing happen to me where I hurt my shoulders a few shoulder surgeries ago, and I went to two doctors. The first doctor went in there, top doctor, put my x-rays up, my MRIs, he goes, mm-hmm, you're screwed, boy, you need a shoulder replacement. Okay, I can tell you, I'm like, whoa, my what? I was so angry at this guy, call my son, this is the worst doctor, how dare you? She's like, come on, I'm like, that was that. Hated the guy. Next day, I had a second appointment and I go to this doctor, walk in, sits down with the guy, he starts putting me through an hour of questions about everything starting from my childhood, early health, how did it start, other things related, all these exams. Within 45 minutes, I'm texting, I'm like, honey, this is the greatest doctor in the world, he's gonna help me out, he's gonna kill my pain, the whole nine yards, he hadn't even given me the prescription yet. But I said, wow, this guy cares, he wants to find out what I really need, and then once he knows it, then he can fill that need. That's what real prospectors do. That's why when someone does an exercise, they say, sell me this pen, it's a, oh, really? How much do you want? How much do you pay for it? What have you sold to you? This person doesn't know the first thing about selling, about getting into rapport. So as we go through now, these lessons on prospecting, just understand that is the context. Sell me this pen is basically a euphemism or an overarching principle that as a straight line salesperson, you would never make a presentation to someone who doesn't need your product, want your product, can be helped by your product, and most importantly or equally important, can afford your product. And unless you're the amazing Crescent of Mind reader, how could you possibly know that without asking them questions? So prospecting equals asking great questions, and then listening in a way where you are, uh-huh, active listening, we'll get to that later, and when you're done and you know what you need to know, say, yes, the person needs my product, they want my product, and they can afford my product, and you say to him, oh, John, you're Jill, based on what you said to me, this is the perfect, right here, this is what I have, they'll say, tell me, what, you're set, it's a hope for greatness. It's the one part of the sale where you can truly show how much you care, because someone who cares asks lots of questions and they listen intently because they want to help you get what you want. That's what prospecting with the straight line is all about. Now, I don't have a quiz for you right now, but there's going to be a quiz after the next lesson. So dive right in and let's rock and roll.
